title: Benchmark Media Console
summary: A 55"-wide plywood media console with a center divider, adjustable shelves, and a pair of acrylic-front doors that let remotes work through a closed cabinet.
description:
A straightforward plywood box, but the acrylic-glass door fronts are what make it worth building over a plain shelf unit — infrared remotes see straight through them, so a cable box or streaming device can live behind closed doors instead of out on the shelf. The center divider splits the cabinet into two roughly equal bays, each with its own shelf and door, so you're really building two smaller cabinets side by side rather than one wide one — which keeps the plywood spans short enough that shelves don't need center support.

Build order matters here: the top glue-up needs a flat clamped surface and time to dry, so start it early and work on the case while it sets. Euro hinges are forgiving to adjust after the fact (that's their whole appeal over surface hinges), so don't agonize over getting the door reveal perfect on the first try — hang it, check the 1/8" gaps, and tweak the hinge cups until it's even.

tools.note: For breaking down the plywood sheets into panels before assembly.
For hanging the doors true and checking the case for square.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: One and a half sheets needed. Rip into 15-3/4" wide strips with the grain for sides, shelves, and top.

materials.name: Pine, 1x6, 6 ft
materials.note: Door panels — cut to fit the actual openings.

materials.name: Acrylic sheet, 1/4" thick, 30" x 24"
materials.note: For the door insert glass — lets remote signals through a closed door.

cutList.note: Cut to fit actual door openings.

steps.title: Break down the sheets and cut the parts
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood into 15-3/4"-wide strips with the grain — three strips come off each 4x8 sheet — then crosscut two sides at 26-1/4", three main shelf and top blanks at 55", one center divider at 17-1/2" and two adjustable shelves at 27-1/8". Those eight panels pack onto four 96" strips (55" plus 26-1/4" twice, then 55" plus 27-1/8", then 27-1/8" plus 17-1/2"), which is exactly the material note's one and a half sheets. From the pine, cut four side trim at 15-3/4", one top back trim at 55" and eight top door area trim at 26-3/4" from the 1x2; six legs at 29-1/4" and four bottom trim at 26-3/4" from the 2x2; and two top breadboard ends at 19-3/4" plus one top front trim at 55" from the 1x3. Leave the doors, the door frame parts and the cabinet back uncut for now — all three are sized to the openings you actually end up with. Label each panel as it comes off the saw, because a 55" shelf and a 55" top blank are indistinguishable once they are stacked.

steps.title: Mark and predrill every pocket hole first
steps.body: Go through the whole plan and mark, then predrill, every pocket hole before you assemble anything — reaching a drill into a half-built cabinet is how corners get skipped. Glue and nail the side trim flush to the top and bottom edges of both side panels.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Through the predrilled pocket holes, attach three legs to each side panel with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Install the two main shelves
steps.body: Mark the shelf locations on both sides and attach with pocket holes. Check the assembly for square before moving on — everything downstream (the divider, the doors) depends on this being right.

steps.title: Attach the center divider
steps.body: Mark the divider's position from both ends rather than measuring once from one side. With a 55" span and a 3/4" divider, each bay works out to 27-1/8" — which is exactly the length of the two adjustable shelves, so if the divider drifts even 1/8" one of those shelves will not go into its bay. Stand the 17-1/2" divider between the two main shelves, square to both, and fasten it with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws top and bottom. That 17-1/2" length is also the clear height of each door opening, so this one measurement carries straight through to the doors.

steps.title: Add bottom and top trim
steps.body: Attach the bottom trim flush on top. Add the top trim to both front and back.

steps.title: Glue up the top
steps.body: Build the top on a flat, level surface, using clamps to keep every joint smooth and even as it dries. A doming or dipping glue joint here shows on the most visible surface of the whole piece.

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: Mark 1" in from the front and both sides and attach the top. If you don't have a right-angle drill, consider installing the doors and hinges before the top goes on — reaching into the tight door openings gets harder once the top is in place.

steps.title: Install the adjustable shelves
steps.body: Fix them permanently with pocket holes, or predrill a row of holes for shelf pins if you want adjustable positions later.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Measure the assembled case across its outside corners and cut the 1/4" plywood back to that size. Do not cut to the cut list's 58" x 26-1/2" figure — it matches neither dimension of a 55"-wide case built on 26-1/4" sides, and cutting to it leaves you long on width and short on height. Square the case first by measuring both diagonals and racking it until they match, then tack the back on with 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue, nailing into both main shelves and the center divider as well as the two sides. The back is what holds the case square permanently, so get the diagonals right before the first nail goes in.

steps.title: Build and hang the doors
steps.body: Measure each door opening for real — nominally 27-1/8" wide by 17-1/2" tall — and build each frame about 1/4" smaller overall than what you measure, because a door built to the plan's numbers instead of your actual openings is the most common reason doors do not hang flush. The cut list's starting point is two 17-1/4" stiles running the door's full height with two 21-1/2" rails fitted between them, which with 2-1/2"-wide stock gives a 26-1/2"-wide door; adjust both figures to your own openings before cutting. Join the frames with pocket screws, check the diagonals, then fasten the acrylic to the back of each frame so it overlaps the frame's inside edge all round. Hang the doors on the Euro hinges, look for an even 1/8" gap on every side, and correct any unevenness at the hinge cups rather than recutting the door — that adjustability is the reason for Euro hinges here.

steps.title: Sand, finish, and add hardware
steps.body: Sand all surfaces, fill nail holes, and finish. Add knobs last, after paint or stain has cured, so you're not sanding around hardware.

images.alt: Benchmark media console with acrylic-front cabinet doors
